scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The verbatim is from the will of Epicurus, whose school was based in Athens; the manumission follows Athenian legal norms.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Epicurus died in -270, the year his will, which contains these manumissions, became legally effective.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The will of Epicurus dictates the maintenance of children within his Athenian-based philosophical community according to local custom.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Epicurus died in -270, which is when the provisions of his will for these dependents would have commenced.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The provision of dowries for orphaned daughters is a specific instruction in the will of Epicurus, an Athenian resident.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The will of Epicurus became effective upon his death in -270, establishing these dowry obligations.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The verbatim discusses the contested citizenship of Epicurus and his father in Athens, which impacted their right to own land.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Epicurus purchased the Garden in Athens around -306, the point at which his status and land-owning rights were most relevant.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Epicurus established these pedagogical rules for his school (the Garden) located in Athens.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: This date represents the establishment of the Epicurean school in Athens, where these educational levels were codified.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The rule regarding advanced study applies to the members of the Epicurean school in Athens.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Epicurus founded his school in Athens in -306, initiating the tiered system of study described.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: This doctrine was formulated by Epicurus within the context of his Athenian school regarding the requirements for wisdom.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to the end of Epicurus's life (-270) as a representative year for his established philosophical doctrines.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Epicurus's ethical doctrines, as cited here, explicitly defer to the laws of the contemporary Athenian state regarding women.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to the floruit/death of Epicurus, whose school operated under these legal assumptions.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The conduct of the 'wise man' toward slaves is a central precept of the Epicurean school in Athens.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to the floruit of Epicurus (-270), who defined the behavior of the ideal wise man in his community.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The right to sue in court refers to the legal standing of the philosopher within the Athenian judicial system.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to the floruit of Epicurus (-270), describing the civic rights exercised by members of his school.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: This is a core invitation to philosophy issued by Epicurus to the youth of the Greek world, specifically centered in Athens.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to the floruit of Epicurus (-270), the author of the letter to Menoeceus containing this rule.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The verbatim describes the institutionalization of the interregnum (interreges) following the death of Romulus.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The first interregnum occurred in -716, immediately following the death/disappearance of Romulus.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Plutarch describes the selection of Numa Pompilius by the Roman and Sabine senators.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Numa Pompilius was elected and began his reign in -715 following the interregnum.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The institution of the Camillus is described by Plutarch as a religious custom established during the early Roman monarchy.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Representative date during the reign of Numa Pompilius (-715 to -673), who is credited with organizing Roman religious life.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Plutarch compares Roman practice specifically to the customs of Greek cities like Delphi and Athens.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: This date represents the classical period of the Greek poleis (Athens and Delphi) mentioned as having this rule.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The term of service for Vestal Virgins was a central part of Numa Pompilius's religious reforms in Rome.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to the early reign of Numa Pompilius, who traditionally established the regulations for the Vestals.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The unique legal status of Vestal Virgins regarding wills and guardianship was a privilege granted by the early Roman state.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to the reign of Numa Pompilius, who is cited as the source of these specific honors and privileges.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The power of pardon for Vestals is described as a fundamental aspect of their sacral authority in early Rome.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to the reign of Numa Pompilius, the traditional founder of the Vestal College and its associated rights.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The disciplinary power of the Pontifex Maximus over the Vestals was established early in Roman history.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to the reign of Numa Pompilius, who established the hierarchies of Roman state religion.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The capital punishment for unchaste Vestals is described as an ancient Roman religious and legal requirement.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to the reign of Numa Pompilius, who codified the laws governing the conduct and punishment of the Vestals.
